Subscriber commands are usually made on process start and end. If it blocks or fails, Ray cannot start properly. IMO using a deadline here compared to not using a deadline can make issues more apparent, e.g. exception for connection failure instead of process hanging. A deadline too small may introduce flakiness if GCS does not respond in time. The default has been no deadline in Ray, e.g. C++ GCS client calls. I think using a longer deadline is safer than a short deadline for now?

Hm. Why is it ok to call pre- and post- hooks on dry-run but not deploy? Because deploy tends to more commonly involve calling out to other servers, whereas pre- and post- tend to be local? As for matching `--dry-run`, thinking about it, it's kind of weird that `certbot renew --dry-run` doesn't run the deploy hook in the first place. I suspect that was less of an active decision and more of a side effect of the implementation path that `--dry-run` uses. I do agree about them matching though, and likewise think your suggestion is a good way to handle a mismatch in behavior.

The issue only happens to Java workers because a Java worker process may include multiple workers. If the number of workers in a worker process is 1, then this code change won't make any difference.

For the multi-worker scenario in Java, please consider this case:

1) A Java worker process with 2 (for simplicity) workers named A and B is considered idle by the worker pool and is about to be killed.
2) The worker pool adds workers A and B to `pending_exit_idle_workers_` and sends `Exit` RPC request to them.
3) Worker A rejects killing and is removed from `pending_exit_idle_workers_`.
4) Somehow worker B didn't reply before the next round of `TryKillingIdleWorkers`.
5) The next round of `TryKillingIdleWorkers` starts.
6) For worker A, the "if" check at line 883 (old) failed because worker A is not in `pending_exit_idle_workers_`. The worker pool considers all workers in the worker process idle and tries to kill worker A and worker B.
6.1) Worker A is added to `pending_exit_idle_workers_` again. An `Exit` RPC request is sent to worker A.
6.2) Worker B is already in `pending_exit_idle_workers_` so the emplace doesn't change anything. An `Exit` RPC request is sent to worker B.
7) Worker A rejects killing and is removed from `pending_exit_idle_workers_`.
8) Now the reply of RPC request sent to worker B at step 2) is received: worker B rejects killing and is removed from `pending_exit_idle_workers_`.
9) The reply of RPC request sent to worker B at step 6.2) is received: worker B rejects killing and **RAY_CHECK failed when removing worker B from `pending_exit_idle_workers_` because it's not in it anymore**.

To fix the potential double removing in edge cases, I moved the `if (pending_exit_idle_workers_.count(worker->WorkerId()))` check into `for (const auto &worker : workers_in_the_same_process)` to skip killing if there's any inflight `Exit` RPC request to this worker process.

Ugh, this does not feel right. Granted, up until recently Pulumi used `go run` therefore ExitStatus was always just either 1 or 2, with the real exit status printed to stderr, so existing programs don't rely on exit codes propagating out. Still. At least Pulumi used to always fail on non-0 exit codes from user code.

This can now swallow exit codes from unrelated conditions quite unexpectedly. 

Can we think of other options? Can we walk through what happens if we remove this and let the func return `return errors.Errorf("program exited with non-zero exit code: %d", status.ExitStatus())`?

If we cannot work this out cleanly, perhaps one crazy option is to overload the exit codes to use a <CUSTOM-INGEGER> reserved by Pulumi to indicate a special exit, that's unlikely to overlap with user code codes.

So, unless we intend users to extend this point, I don't think that this should be a `@union`.

The things that a user can safely do at this point are pretty limited: you can essentially only execute readonly operations on the lockfile... otherwise you would invalidate the header, and we wouldn't be able to tell that we needed to regenerate it.

If #17347 adds an option (or default!) to render the diff after regenerating the lockfile, that doesn't need to be behind a `@union`: if after using that feature a user wants to extend this point and add more ways to render the diff, then maybe we could add an extension point... otherwise it feels like premature generalization.

The one thing I'm a _little_ worried about here is just that this file wasn't originally intended to be permalinked-to, so it might change at some point, and then this link would break.

One way to fix this is by referring to that file at a specific commit hash, e.g.:

https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JustFixNYC/nycha-scraper/098bd8232bee2cd59266bf278e26e32bd0cd7df1/Block-and-Lot-Guide-08272018.csv

That's the latest version of the file at https://github.com/JustFixNYC/nycha-scraper/commit/098bd8232bee2cd59266bf278e26e32bd0cd7df1, and if for some reason we decide to remove it from the repository, or rename it, or something else, nycdb won't break.  The downside is that if we ever somehow improve on the dataset (e.g. we find a bug in the scraping logic and re-scrape the PDF) we'd have to remember to also update the revision that this URL points to.  But I feel like that's less risky than accidentally breaking NYCDB.

I considered that approach, but it's hard for the application to know how many `otLinkedBuffer`s to use.

But I think having the system allocate `otLinkedBuffer`s is not very hard. If we implement the receive queue as a queue of `otMessage`s, then we follow your suggestion of including an `otLinkedBuffer` in `otMessageBuffer`. Then this problem goes away.

If we use the approach I suggested, which is to use a flat circular buffer for the receive buffer (in order to avoid `otMessage` livelock) then the system needs at most two `otLinkedBuffer`s to reference the data in the circular buffer. These two `otLinkedBuffer`s can just be fields in `otTcpActiveSocket`.

So in summary I don't think it's difficult for the TCP implementation to allocate these `otLinkedBuffer`s.

> New function works a little differently from how it was before

It doesn't really make a difference in the end. The sum of the "old" vs "new" array is 1008 vs 1001, meaning that the new function just additionally checks for axis bounds. It wasn't required before as these lengths were only used to split the frame via `.iloc` which doesn't fail on out-of-bounds slices, but since now we use these lengths as the actual partitions lengths/widths it's mandatory for them to not be out of bounds.

> Also, your function creates an intermediate list, which looks slower. Is this slowdown insignificant?

Running `split_result_of_axis_func_pandas` 1_000 times gives the following cumulative number of seconds:
|master|this PR|
|-|-|
|10.12s|10.44s|

So yeah, it's a bit slower, but I don't think it's significant as given this number the difference for a single call is 0.0003 seconds.


<details><summary>Testing code</summary>

```python
from timeit import timeit
from modin.core.storage_formats.pandas.utils import split_result_of_axis_func_pandas
import pandas

NROWS = 10_000
NCOLS = 64
NSPLITS = 1024

df = pandas.DataFrame({f"col{i}": range(NROWS) for i in range(NCOLS)})
print(
    timeit(
        lambda: split_result_of_axis_func_pandas(axis=0, num_splits=NSPLITS, result=df),
        number=1_000,
    )
)
```

</details>

It seems that this case is even more interesting. When grouping on an index level and performing reducing aggregation, the `as_index=False` parameter resets the index of the result to the default one without inserting anything to the actual frame:

<details><summary>Example</summary>

```python
>>> df
  by  col1  col2
0  a     1     3
1  a     2     4
2  b     3     5
3  b     4     6
>>> df.set_index("by").groupby(level=0, as_index=False).sum()
   col1  col2
0     3     7
1     7    11
>>> df.set_index("by").groupby(level=0, as_index=False).nunique()
   col1  col2
0     2     2
1     2     2
>>> df.set_index("by").groupby(level=0, as_index=False).apply(lambda df: df.sum())
   col1  col2
0     3     7
1     7    11
```

</details>

But when applying a map-aggregation, the `as_index=False` parameter has just no effect, because it always trying to preserve the index of the original frame:
<details><summary>Example</summary>

```python
>>> df.set_index("by").groupby(level=0, as_index=False).apply(lambda df: df + 1)
    col1  col2
by
a      2     4
a      3     5
b      4     6
b      5     7
>>> df.set_index("by").groupby(level=0, as_index=False).shift()
    col1  col2
by
a    NaN   NaN
a    1.0   3.0
b    NaN   NaN
b    3.0   5.0
>>> df.set_index("by").groupby(level=0, as_index=False).fillna(0)
    col1  col2
by
a      1     3
a      2     4
b      3     5
b      4     6
# Grouping only on a single index level, but both are presented in the result
>>> df.set_index(["by", "col1"]).groupby(level=0, as_index=False).fillna(0)
         col2
by col1
a  1        3
   2        4
b  3        5
   4        6
```

</details>

A proper solution would be is to follow the pandas logic: always reset index when grouping on index level and restore it only for a certain non-reducing aggregations at the API layer. But this approach may slow-down groupby for map-aggregations since frame relabeling might be quite expensive (especially if the frame has a lot of delayed computations). 

Another solution could be is to deduce somehow whether the aggregation is reducing or not at the place where we're dealing with `as_index` (at the QC), for example, we can check, whether the length of the result has changed after aggregation was applied (but it looks like an easy-breakable solution).

I'd like to keep the logic of handling `as_index` in one place, not to split it between backend and front-end (ideally, we want to see the whole `as_index` logic at the front, since this parameter is a tricky one and we don't want to delegate such pandas-dependent task to the backend), that's why I'm complaining about the current solution. But let's wait for other reviewers' opinions, maybe it's an acceptable compromise.

BTW, the first steps of moving the handling of `as_index` to the front were made in #3592, where I encapsulate the trickiest of `as_index` processing in a single backend-agnostic function, that actually could be used at the front-end. You can take a look at it, maybe you would have some suggestions of how to modify it in order to support these grouping-on-index-level cases.

@jinran-google I fully agree with Simon, the platform layer should not access internal to core definition/methods (in few cases this rule is broken which is unfortunate and we need to address). I would also not recommend using `otLinkSetSupportedChannelMask()` for this as that is a public OT API and is intended for use by next layer above Thread stack rather than the platform layer. 

Here's what I suggest:

- We already have `otPlatRadioGetSupportedChannelMask()` as a radio platform API in `radio.h` which should return the supported channel mask to OT core (from platform layer).
- You can update of `otPlatRadioGetSupportedChannelMask()` in `posix/platfomr/radio.cpp` to update what it returns based on the region (get the mask from RCP and then filter it more based the region).
- The challenge with this is how to indicate to OT core that channel mask is now changed. 
  - We can add a callback in `radio.h` like `extern void otPlatRadioChannelMaskChanegd(otInstance *aInstance);
` to indicate to OT that channel mask is changed, so that OT can get it back from radio (that would be useful if we expect there may be other ways that the mask may change on radio).
  - The simpler way can be to have this happen in core itself, when `Radio::SetRegionCode()` method is called, it calls a method in `Mac` to notify it that channel mask may have changed and then `Mac` would query radio to get it.

However, in general the addition of `otPlatRadioSetRegion()` makes it rather complicated. 
- If device is already commissioned and operating on a channel (with a set channel mask in , how should we behave if the region is changed causing the channel we are operating on now invalid).

I've now pushed changes that use the already existing KMS mock. These changes have the aforementioned downside that they expose `kmsiface.KMSAPI` variable in `kms` package in order to make it accessible from `keyservice` package. It would be simpler to have this new encryption context test located within `kms` package to allow it to access that `kmsiface.KMSAPI` mock without publicly exporting the variable, but this would lead to a circular dependency between the packages (`kms` -> `keyservice` -> `kms`). 

In my opinion, it would be preferable to consider getting rid of the use of a global variable for mock injection purposes and instead refactor the `kms` package to allow tests to provide the `kmsiface.KMSAPI` implementation directly to an instance of `kms.MasterKey`. This would remove the need to make the variable public for mocking purposes. Additionally, I'm wondering if the global variable might cause any kind of concurrency issues between tests. I'm not that well-versed in Go to know how concurrency might influence these testing scenarios, but I can see that if two tests were running concurrently and attempted to modify the global `kmsiface.KMSAPI`, there would be trouble.

> 
> **This function does nothing if the root logger already has handlers configured**, unless the keyword argument force is set to True.

- We have `mState = kStateNewServiceData;` which indicates there is some pending change we need to do the sync (and we can enter this from any other state on `ServerDataUpdated()` call or `HandleStateChanged()` call).

- From  `HandleCoapResponse()` we use the `mNextDelay` to restart the timer to do next round of `SynchronizeNetworkData()`.

It bothers me that this command is "simply" a part of the `before_script`, because:
- it is in fact the main purpose of this pipeline, and should benefit from a correct visibility in the pipeline for troubleshooting purpose
- it will run for each associated task, so one for the test task, and one for the snap task, while ideally, it should be an artifact that is reused on all tasks that needs it
- for multiarch build, this overhead will become very high
- failure to build the snap is a fail fast condition here: associated to the multiple call of snap builds described above, we will start several time the same broken build, wasting resources and time during the release and the nightly builds

In general I think this should be a dedicated stage, that would be before the `test` one. But this implies to be able to store pipeline artifacts, and Travis CI does not support that (it has only the capability to push artifacts to external storages, see https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/uploading-artifacts/). So I do not really have a solution here, but wanted to note that the situation is suboptimal, if you have an alternative in mind.

I see. Before, we would use DFLT (implicitly, if declared in global languagesystems) if we had _no_ LTR vs RTL lookup split, or explicitly if we did have two split LTR and RTL lookups but only one set of scripts (either LTR or RTL) was declared in global languagesystems. With this PR, we only use DFLT for the kern_Common lookup containing pairs with undefined script.

The consequence of the change, I suppose, is that kerning would stop working if a text's script isn't set up correctly (e.g. a run of latin mistakenly marked as greek or whatever).
I don't know how desirable it would be to continue supporting the use of DFLT script, at least in the simple scenario when there is only one script-specific lookup (e.g. kern_Latn in addition perhaps to kern_Common). We could in the latter case make a single unsplit `kern` lookup containing all the pairs and don't declare any script/language explicitly so it would be registered under all globally languagesystems, including DFLT (the latter is recommended by the FEA spec and almost always declared).

> **Note:** When you nest patch decorators the mocks are passed in to the decorated function in the same order they applied (the normal Python order that decorators are applied). This means from the bottom up, so in the example above the mock for module.ClassName1 is passed in first.

```python
>>> import mock

>>> @mock.patch('requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter.cert_verify')
... @mock.patch('requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter.get_connection')
... def test_requests(get_connection_mock, cert_verify_mock):
...     print('get_connection_mock: {}'.format(get_connection_mock))
...     print('cert_verify_mock: {}'.format(cert_verify_mock))
...     

>>> test_requests()
get_connection_mock: <MagicMock name='get_connection' id='139800850314296'>
cert_verify_mock: <MagicMock name='cert_verify' id='139800850164928'>
```

The reason it's implemented this way is because `read_hash_bytes()` is called many times in succession to read all of the hashes for the children of a node (i.e. via the `read_node_hash_bytes()` method, which in turn is called via `Trie::get_children_hashes_bytes()`).  The call is meant to _append_ the bytes to the given buffer, which `fast_extend_from_slice()` accelerates by way of avoiding a `malloc()`.

`read_block_parent()` is only called when loading the fork table into memory, which happens once one the peer initializes.  It's not great that it's inefficient, but it's not a huge performance penalty either.

Since there are so many variable names for these strings, it ironically makes it harder to figure out what the actual global variable is actually called: `KOLIBRI_JS_PLUGIN_DATA_NAME`, `__kolibriPluginData`, `kolibriPluginDataName`, `__kolibriPluginDataName`... but all the dev wants to do is inspect it in their console.

I think in this case, it would be preferable to lower the amount of indirection and allow the strings to be defined more than once in the codebase. If the strings are unique, meaningful, and unlikely to change, there's not a huge win from having a single source of truth. So for example:

* `window.theKolibriPluginData`
* `window.theKolibriCoreApp`

These are unique, easily-searched strings and use the [singleton naming convention](https://vuejs.org/v2/style-guide/#Single-instance-component-names-strongly-recommended). Also since they're named similarly, they'll be clustered and easy to find in the dev tools.

Filename matching has been a sore spot but I can't think of a better solution. There is some metadata in the filenames that is just "nice to have" but provides a better picture of the data being loaded. There is some that is necessary and not available in the file contents. However, the main point here is that it is needed because there is no better *easy* way to sort files for what python code should parse them. You either:

1. Sort by filename and give the filename to a class to read the files (what we do now).
2. Open the file, check some global attribute(s), then use that information to decide how the file should be read. *BUT* that requires knowing how to open the file in the first place. Not all files are netcdf or hdf5. Well then, you say, we could have code that looks at the extension of the file and determines if it knows how to read it. Then we are back to option 1 as some filenames don't have extensions or need to be decompressed in a certain way or sometimes they are even mis-extension'd.

We need to be able to determine what these filenames are *fast*. Having to open the files in any way isn't really an option. Having the user provide the information while possible, is not something non-programmer users can do. It also gets a little difficult when dealing with a lot of files. It ends up requiring the user to know low-level things about the files they are trying to read. At least I think so.

Currently, I don't think the owner keeps a {`TaskId` -> `TaskSpecification`} mapping for the tasks that are in-flight to a worker. In fact, right after calling `PushNormalTask` to push a task to a worker, `OnWorkerIdle` pops the task's TaskSpec from the relevant owner's queue. So once we push a task, the owner will not see the task's TaskSpec until the worker responds to the owner and activates the `PushNormalTask` callback (which captures the TaskSpec).

For this reason, when we perform work stealing, the only way for the owner to access the the stolen tasks' TaskSpecs is for the victim to send them back along with the `StealWork` rpc reply. 

The worker will eventually also respond to each of the stolen tasks' original `PushNormalTask` RPCs, so an alternative approach would be to let those `PushNormalTask` callbacks take care of pushing the stolen tasks to the thief. However, this approach would be less efficient, because the replies would only start coming back after the worker has finished executing the non-stolen tasks that were pushed to the worker's queue before the stolen ones.

@Ramshackle-Jamathon I was thinking something in this tone:

### How easy is this going to be, really?

You may be coming to this document/library in a skeptical frame of mind, having previously been burned by projects that claim to have 100% foolproof, automated migration tools. We want to assure you that we are _not_ making that claim.

The `dagster-airflow` migration library should be viewed as a powerful _accelerant_ of migration, rather than guaranteeing completely _automated_ migration. The amount of work required is proportional to the complexity of your installation. Smaller implementations of less complexity can be trivial to migrate, making it appear virtually automatic; larger, more complicated, or customized implementations require more investment. 

For larger installations, teams that already adopt devops practices and/or have standardized usage of Airflow will have a smoother transition. 

Some concrete examples:

* If you rely on the usage of the Airflow UI to set production connections or variables, this will require a change of workflow as you will no longer have the Airflow UI at the end of this migration. If instead you rely on code-as-infrastructure patterns to set connections or variables, migration more straightforward. 
* If you have standardized on a small set or even a single operator type (e.g. the K8sPodOperator), migration will be easiser. If you use a large number of operator types with a wide range of infrastructure requirements, migration will be more work.
* If you dynamically generate your Airflow DAGs from a higher-level API or DSL (e.g. yaml), the migration will be more straightforward than all your stakeholders directly creating Airflow DAGs.

Even in the case that requires some infrastructure investment, `dagster-airflow` dramatically eases migration, by orders of magnitude. The cost can be borne by a single or small set of infrastructure-oriented engineers, and it dramatically reduces coordination costs, as you do not have to move all of your stakeholders over to new APIs simultaneously. In our experience, practitioners welcome the change, because of the immediate improvement in tooling, stability, and development speed.

> why it can't just invoke state APIs in the loop
Our APIs are currently all blocking, so invoking APIs in a loop will block the loop. A simpler alternative would be using a threaded actor, but since most of the blocking happens at network, I feel it fits the async model better. 

Most of the complexity comes from running the API in another async thread to prevent blocking. If we have async APIs, it will be much easier. 

I also think "invoking a blocking call periodically but async waiting for its results" could be a generic pattern so I went for some over-engineering here.

Regarding this point:

> So the code here suppose to get the chunk_end value, becouse we want to stop reading the VCF as soon as we get through the target region. To do this, in my opinion, we have 3 solution.

If the binning index gives the end offset, then this sounds like a reasonable option to figure out when to stop reading. To be honest I still don't fully understand the binning index, so I cannot make a perfect judgement here.

An alternative I think is to use the linear index to find the offset to start reading, and then just keep reading until the parser finds a record which starts beyond the query end. I.e., let the underlying parser figure out when to stop reading. 

Currently the parser does check whether the current variant record starts beyond the end of the query region, [code here](https://github.com/cggh/scikit-allel/blob/655254106c09a23ba9c6dc40c83b4a443a66bcfc/allel/opt/io_vcf_read.pyx#L1059). However, that code currently is designed for the case where a VCF is not sorted (yes that happens) and so it only skips the variant, it does not terminate parsing. However, we could pass down a piece of information that the VCF is sorted (which we know it will be if querying via tabix index), and the parser could then use this to terminate parsing rather than just skipping the record.  

In other words, I *think* it would be possible to just use the linear index to find a suitable file offset to start reading from, and then let the parser figure out when to stop reading based on parsing records.

I had considered the same, and I think it would be a good idea to move them all over to `file_metadata_provider.py` as part of this PR.

From the perspective of an end-user, they should be able to use `from ray.data.datasource import FastFileMetadataProvider` regardless of which file we put the class inside of, so this shouldn't change much from their perspective.

From the perspective of a Ray Data maintainer, the current organization largely grew out of organic growth of the code, where one-off utility functions have gradually evolved into more generic/extensible classes over time, and thus follow existing conventions of grouping utility class impls with the datasource that uses them. Thus, all file metadata providers given in `file_based_datasource.py` are meant to be used with file-based datasources, while all file metadata providers in `parquet_datasource.py` are meant to be used specifically with the `ParquetDatasource`.

The primary downside of continuing to preserve this type of grouping is that core dependencies like `file_based_datasource.py` starts to grow too large over time (sitting now at >700 LoC), which makes it harder to grok the important parts at a glance or to understand during a quick top-to-bottom read. 

If we were to move all file metadata provider implementations into `file_metadata_provider.py`, I'd expect it to be just over 300 LoC with all classes organized around the central purpose of providing file metadata. This better normalizes the size of our "large files" and keeps them more focused on a single purpose, so I'm slightly in favor of making this move now if we're in agreement that it's beneficial.

We probably also should do the same with block write path providers in a follow-up PR.

After thinking about it for a moment: it kind-of makes sense that it can happen in a capsule destructor (objects are deleted while exceptions unwind). I think to avoid that we need to chain the errors with `raise_from`.

Ideally the complex error handling should only be done from the destructors, not from the plain `name()` member function. It's a bug if `name()` is called while `PyErr_Occurred()` is true, unless it is from a destructor.

In such case, `result` is a [`LROPoller`](https://github.com/Azure/msrest-for-python/blob/master/msrest/polling/poller.py#L103) whose `polling_method` is [`NoPolling`](https://github.com/Azure/msrest-for-python/blob/master/msrest/polling/poller.py#L66) whose [`finished()`](https://github.com/Azure/msrest-for-python/blob/master/msrest/polling/poller.py#L91) method always returns `True`. 

If you want to force `LongRunningOperation` to wait here, you need to remove `sdk_no_wait`. For example, during VM creation:

blob_* methods takes the input image of dimensionality N and applies a gaussian filter with 7 different sigmas, and makes an image of dimensionality N+1.  Then it applies peak_local_max across that composite image.  however, since `exclude_border` was previously _not_ anistropic and was actually ignored if a footprint was provided, it ended up zeroing out a bunch of the gaussian images.

example:

- input image is (500,500)
- blob_* takes the image and applies gaussian filter with varying sigmas, and produces a (7, 500, 500) image.
- previous behavior of exclude_border + footprint erroneously zeroes [0, :, :], [1, :, :], [5, :, :], and [6, :, :].

This resulted in `blob_log` finding no spots, not as a result of removing the border, but as a result of erroneously zeroing out data.

This example is confusing. It looks like it is a complete test script example, but actually the intent is not to provide a full example here as in the other api commands. 
The reason for the confusion is, I think, putting everything in the same presentation block.
Separating each excerpt in its unique presentation block with a one sentence explanation/comment is going to solve the problem:

The following shows the required module(s) for `copy_object` api to work, that is `minio.commonconfig`.
 `datetime` module is only needed if a time related conditional arg, like `modified_since` or `unmodified_since`, is used. So, It is better to show them together with the "modified condition"ed copy object examples.
```
from minio import Minio
# copy_object api needs minio.commonconfig module to be imported
from minio.commonconfig import CopySource

client = Minio(
    "s3.amazonaws.com",
    access_key="YOUR-ACCESSKEY",
    secret_key="YOUR-SECRETKEY",
)

# Copy object "my-source-bucketname/my-source-objectname" to "my-bucketname/my-objectname"
client.copy_object(
    "my-bucketname",
    "my-objectname",
    CopySource("my-source-bucketname", "my-source-objectname"),
)
```
```
# Copy object "my-source-bucketname/my-source-objectname", if its version id matches the provided
# version id 
:
:
client.copy_object(
    "my-bucketname",
    "my-objectname",
    CopySource(
        "my-source-bucketname", "my-source-objectname",
        version_id="b6602757-7c9c-449b-937f-fed504d04c94",
    ),
)
```
```
# Copy object "my-source-bucketname/my-source-objectname", if its "modified-since" 
# value is later than or equal to the provided modified date and time and its etag value matches the
# provided etag value.
# Print the object name if the object is copied over successfully,
# otherwise an error message about the failed condition.
:
from datetime import datetime, timezone
:
:
result = client.copy_object(
    "my-bucketname",
    "my-objectname",
    CopySource(
        "my-source-bucketname",
        "my-source-objectname",
        match_etag="31624deb84149d2f8ef9c385918b653a",
        modified_since=datetime(2014, 4, 0, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
    ),
)
print(result.object_name, result.version_id)
```
```
# Copy object "my-source-bucketname/my-source-objectname", and
# replace its meta data with the provided meta data (key: value) pair.
# Add meta data (key: value) pair, if there is no meta data with the provided key name, `X-Amz-Meta-<key>`
:
from minio.commonconfig import REPLACE, CopySource
:
:
metadata = {"test_meta_key": "test_meta_value"}
result = client.copy_object(
    "my-bucket",
    "my-object",
    CopySource("my-sourcebucket", "my-sourceobject"),
    metadata=metadata,
    metadata_directive=REPLACE,
)
```

> The timer is started in HandleTransmitDone instead of BeginTransmit right? Is that ok? 

Yes. Correct. We want to add delay before we start the next retx which will have its own CSMA process (which may be done by radio platform if it supports it or if not will be done by `SubMac` code in `StartCsmaBackoff()`.  In the existing code, when we determine that frame still needs to be retx, we would immediately call `StartCsmaBackoff()` from here (and in the case radio does the CSMA, `StartCsmaBackoff()` would directly call `BeginTransmit()`).

> In case of getting a Data Poll is there a possibility an indirect frame can be sent in-between direct frame retransmissions.

That is not possible (with the implementation in general). `SubMac` layer deals with one frame tx request at a time and then it is up to the next layer (`Mac` and other layer) to deal with data poll and direct vs indirect messages. 
`Mac` itself has the concept of `Operation`. At any point a single `Operation` is active and being executed. It keeps track of other requests and remembers the pending operations. Once the current operation is finished, it determines the highest priority next operation to start with.

I’ve been thinking about this a bit…

I note that the top-level `_RequestBuilder` is “pull-based” in the sense
that it takes an iterator and produces an iterator via a coroutine. This
fits in nicely with the surrounding code on both sides: the client just
iterates over the requests from the request builder, and the request
builder itself (inside the coroutine) iterates over the events from the
logdir loader.

The new `_ScalarRequestBuilder`, on the other hand, is “push-based”: it
receives a single event at a time and must immediately decide what
requests to emit and whether to put-back the event. From a different
perspective, both request builders conceptually have a loop, but the
loop in `_RequestBuilder` is internal while `_ScalarRequestBuilder` loop
is external.

I wonder if it could be cleaner and more uniform if everything were
pull-based. At a conceptual level it makes sense to me to think of the
`_RequestBuilder` as splitting its input stream into three—one stream
each for just the scalar, tensor, and blob sequence `Value`s—and
delegating to `_[DataClass]RequestBuilder`s, each of which takes a
single stream of events and returns a single stream of requests, just as
before. In particular, the “peeking” behavior would be pushed down into
the `_[DataClass]RequestBuilder`s, so `_ScalarRequestBuilder` would
actually be exactly the same as `_RequestBuilder` was before this PR.
Then, the top-level `_RequestBuilder` would multiplex over these three
streams and return a single stream of their mixed responses.

In fact, `LogdirLoader` itself does something similar, multiplexing over
directory loaders, and directory loaders themselves multiplex over
timestamped event file loaders, which in turn abstract over a couple
more layers.

Here’s a summary picture to better convey what I mean:
<https://gist.github.com/wchargin/c123db42927b93d7ebf4d9793a555b9a#gistcomment-3167958>

So I’m curious—did you consider an approach like this? and what do you
think?

(For context, I went through a surprisingly large number of iterations
of this code and similar batching code in the backend, and it seemed to
all fall out more nicely with a pull-based approach. That doesn’t mean
that this will continue to be the case, though.)

https://github.com/googlefonts/ufo2ft/blob/ad28eea062e0dd48678309bd9ef86dfcc85fa85a/tests/featureWriters/kernFeatureWriter_test.py#L691-L695

Whereas here, it looks like the `kerningRTL` dict only contains the RTL kerning pairs.

The issue with doing a `multiple_link_or_merge` and then a `link_or_merge` with the `source` being `end_body` is that since it has no statements it will be discarded as its predecessors will be directly linked to its successors after the `multiple_link_or_merge` call. This is why everything linked after the body is in one `after_body` list. 

Now, to avoid having the `else_block` being created regardless of what `node.orelse` evaluates to, I had to move the `multiple_link_or_merge` call to right after the `node.orelse` `if` block. This creates a problem highlighted in the following example:

```
try: // 1
    pass
except:
    pass
else:
    try:  // 2
        pass
    else:
        pass
```
When the visitor method is called on the `try/except` block marked by the comment `// 2`, it will create a new block and `link_or_merge` it with the previous one. In this particular example, the previous block will be the empty `else_block` that will be discarded because of the `link_or_merge` operation. Then when the visitor method continues evaluating the `try/except` block marked by the comment `// 1`, `end_body` will be linked the discarded `else_block`. This is solved by this fix:

https://github.com/pyta-uoft/pyta/blob/6bb79f1bba37702ca3a676aaafec9bb8b4cb7c18/python_ta/cfg/visitor.py#L204

Which in turn motivates this:

https://github.com/pyta-uoft/pyta/blob/6bb79f1bba37702ca3a676aaafec9bb8b4cb7c18/python_ta/cfg/graph.py#L145-L146

Which then motivates me the replace every `CFGBlock.statement == []` simply with `not CFGBlock`. I think that a block with no statements is similar to an empty list and so this refactoring makes sense. If you say so then I'll make a PR for this right away.

I think my comment was slightly misleading because I was referring to a future situation where we constrain the target platform. This example should work under the current setup.    The flow would look something like this:
* rule emits an MPEPR with multiple requests.
* (command runner is the local runner) command_runner::get_compatible_requests returns  the request which can run on the local platform.
* request is missing in the cache so the local command runner is used to actually run the request.
* result is cached under the composite key.
* (user connects to web and turns on remoting / speculation) previously run rule emits the same MPEPR
* MPEPR is still compatible, (perhaps now with the remote cluster), command_runner::run is called
* cache computes the same composite key, and previously computed result is returned.

If the aggregator is installed on an existing instance, then it's sometimes desirable to fill "Aggregate" data for the existing courses. There is the [reaggregate_course](https://github.com/open-craft/openedx-completion-aggregator/blob/master/completion_aggregator/management/commands/reaggregate_course.py) management command, which prepares data that will be aggregated during the next `run_aggregator_service` run.
However that are a few points that might cause problems while doing this if you are running it for all courses on a big instance. People deploying this should be sure that they have:
1. Disabled cronjobs (if they have already enabled them). Otherwise these will clog Redis.
1. Enough computing power to run the aggregator for the first time. For our case we did a simulation by moving all data from prod to stage and measuring how long this will take. For one instance they were taking about 125h, so for the prod [we went with](https://tasks.opencraft.com/browse/BB-2296?focusedCommentId=148978&page=com.atlassian.jira.plugin.system.issuetabpanels:comment-tabpanel#comment-148978) disabling all existing Celery workers and spawning 3 new `m5.xlarge` instances running only workers with concurrency set to 4 (so 12 processes total).
1. Enough DB computing power. We have upgraded our RDS for this process, to be sure that the heavy parallelization won't make LMS unusable.
1. Enough DB storage (described [here](https://forum.opencraft.com/t/opencraft-ops-review/533/5)). "Aggregate" data is quite heavy - for us it added 20GB to the DB size during the initial run. We had RDS auto scaling enabled, but it throttled the instance significantly. Rescaling it manually fixed the issue, so it might be worth to scale DB upfront.

Notice that `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CSL_RECEIVE_TIME_AHEAD` can be tuned by the platform implementation in order to invoke `ReceiveAt` well in advance of the actual receive operation, so there is time for the current transmission operation to finish. And in any case it's responsibility of the radio platform to handle possible conflicts with the delayed operations.

> Both config and stack are keyed by stack name, which makes the grouping feel a bit odd. The config belongs to a stack and the relationship with workspace is only really for persistence. Not sure if this subgrouping over-emphasizes the relationship between config and workspace.

Agreed. Yeah, my initial factoring isn't quite right: if we were to go down this path, the config-related things would probably be better on `Stacks`, but even then it doesn't really make it clear that this is config for the current stack.

In any case, apologies for taking us on this tangent. Let's ignore my grouping idea for now and go back to discussing the previous naming suggestions on the top-level workspace:

### Current naming:

- `GetConfigAsync` for a single `ConfigValue`.
- `GetAllConfigAsync` for the config map.

### Option 1 (@orionstudt's original suggestion):

- `GetConfigValueAsync` for a single `ConfigValue`.
- `GetConfigAsync` for the config map.

### Option 2:

- `GetConfigValueAsync` (singular) for a single `ConfigValue`.
- `GetConfigValuesAsync` (plural) for the config map.

Which is similar to the built-in [`Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.environment.getenvironmentvariable?view=net-5.0) and [`Environment.GetEnvironmentVariables`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.environment.getenvironmentvariables?view=net-5.0).

### Option 3:

For languages that support overloads (C#, TypeScript, Python), we could have two overloads with the same name (e.g. `GetConfigAsync`). The overload that accepts just the `stackName` returns all config, the overload that accepts both the `stackName` and `key` returns just the value:

```c#
Task<ImmutableDictionary<string, ConfigValue>> GetConfigAsync(string stackName);
Task<ConfigValue> GetConfigAsync(string stackName, string key);
```

Go doesn't support overloads, so if we move to this approach, we'd need to either keep Go's naming as-is, or rename based on the other naming suggestions above. Or another possible option for Go is `GetConfig` (single value) and `GetConfigMap` (all config).

---

Personally, I'm in favor of Option 1 (@orionstudt's original suggestion). If we do this, we should be prepared to change the naming for TS and Go for consistency.

For most of the values, we want to handle the assertion as per `assertions.assertEqualSimple()` as its able to either pass `assert` for matching values, raise `AssertionError` for mismatches or omit the `assert` if one of the values has `PASS` in it. This works for strings and sequences: 
 - `"a_value"` vs. `"a_value"` - this should pass the `assert`
 - `"a_value"` vs. `"mismatch"` - this should raise `AssertionError`
 - `"a_value"` vs. `"PASS"` - should omit the assert, and not raise `AssertionError`
 - `["a_value", "another_value"]` vs. `["PASS"]` - should omit the assert, and not raise `AssertionError`

For booleans however, we have no means to signal to `assertEqualSimple()` that we intend to omit the `assert`, as the value might not be implemented yet. Furthermore, `assertions.isPass()` that gets called by `assertions.assertEqualSimple()` does `return PASS in value`, which obviously doesn't work for booleans.

That said, we could handle omitting the checks for booleans in `assertions.assertEqualSimple()` as well.

Two questions/concerns here:

1. Could we support incremental fitting without a new `.update()` API by letting `.fit()` accept `DatasetPipelines`?
2. This incremental fitting won't work if `.fit()` finalizes aggregations whose partial results are unmergeable, e.g. stddev for the standard scaler. If `.fit()` no longer finalizes aggregations, we'll then have to figure out when that finalization happens (should there be a `.finalize()` API?)

If we still want incremental fitting on plain Datasets, IMO it might be a cleaner API to have `.fit()` always finalize, and have a `.partial_fit()` API that's meant for incremental fitting, similar to sklearn, + a `.partial_finalize()` API to finalize/"seal" the fit.

Note that all of this can be handled under-the-hood of the `.fit()` API if we go with (1).

The only places it's used for determining behaviour is when dealing with RX tasks, but they're stored in separate lists so they won't affect each other (and `python-can` currently doesn't support this).

But yes, you're right, it should be safe to increase this to 32-bits.

I see, having the Response extend the Spec doesn't make sense, since the former type belongs to the DataSource, while the latter belongs to NgRx store.

> SourceFileResponse is determined by the server. I don't want it too be too entangled the ngrx data structure here

`SourceFileResponse` defined by the server, already has to depend (implicitly) on a type belonging to the NgRx store.  If `SourceFileSpec`'s fields were renamed or changed, we would break unless `SourceFileResponse` was updated in the same way.  And vice versa.

I think perhaps what we need is to move the `SourceFileSpec` type into a DataSource's types.  Then, it would make sense for the NgRx to depend on DataSource, and not the other way around.

```yaml
channels:

channel_settings:
  - channel: channel
    auth: conda-auth-oauth2
    login_url: http://localhost:8041/login
```

```
conda create -n tester channel::travis_test
```

Slices are already reference types (kind-of; they're triples of pointer, length, and capacity, but they're nilable so for our purposes, they're reference types[^1]), so they're always used and passed by value.
APIs in Go almost never deal with pointers to slices—except in odd cases when you want to, say, modify the slice variable someone else owns. This is similar to using, say `ok = combine(x, y, &result)` in C—to get output from a function into a pointer—and not a thing that's normally done otherwise.

So, less convention and more idiom/correctness. Slices, maps, and interfaces are always passed by value because that's the only way you can consume them. 

[^1]: If you're curious about the internals, https://go.dev/blog/slices-intro has a run-down. I also have a blog post [here](https://abhinavg.net/2019/07/11/zero-alloc-slice-filter/) that tries to explain it with a different example.

comparison between current implementation sequence and `next`:

### Current
- LightGBM determines how to read, in-order out-of-order etc
- Data Provides hints reading behavior via `batch_size`
##### Potential improvements
- generally prefetching, ultimate form be like [auto-data-tunning ](https://www.tensorflow.org/guide/data_performance) in TensorFlow 
- parallel reading, even distributed reading
- natural to go with MPI (MPI I/O) Interface 

### `next`
- simplicity 
- data provider get to control what and how to read
- data provider could optimize data feeding accordingly (no need to wait for two segment of data from different cassette be fully loaded, just early cut when one is loaded and hint an prefetch event) 
##### future
- total separation of sampling and data reading stages, each could benefits in someway
- infinite dataset support 


To compare:
- both changes to current implementation and `next` approach could improve efficiency upon current implemntation
- current being more general, could leverage speed for almost any data source once sophisticated prefetch mechanism are implemented
- `next` being simplistic, giving all efficiency rights to data provider (note, data providers are still not limited in current implementation)

And yes, I chose to use the `deferParam` system because `remove`/`auto_remove` doesn't work well with `detach=True`. So for example: 

```python
def docker_call(job):
    container = apiDockerCall(job,
                              image="ubuntu:latest",
                              parameters=["ls", "-lha", "/"],
                              detach=True,
                              remove=True,
                              )

    stdout = container.logs(stderr=False, stdout=True)  # 404 error!
    logger.warning(stdout)
```

Here, you'd get a `docker.errors.NotFound: 404 Client Error: Not Found ("No such container: "...")` error, because I think the container is removed before the `logs()` call. I'd be curious to know if there's a way to solve this? 

Using the `deferParams=RM` system, however, would call `docker kill` and `docker rm` after users are done with the container, so I think that would give the desired behavior? I originally planned to use the `remove` flag for clean up when `detach=False`, and defer remove when `detach=True`, but that seemed to complicate things more. 

I'm leaning towards fixing the default `deferParams` behavior, but let me know what you think.

The new implementation is lazy, in the sense that each setting is loaded only when it's value is queried. The former implementation was lazy in the sense that it loaded *all* settings as soon as *one* setting value was queried. The `Settings` object might be loaded multiple times, but that's ok, because creating new objects is free.

So in terms of pure performance, I would argue that the new implementation is more efficient. Also, it is probably more correct as as well, as it takes into account the cases when setting values change (a rare use case, but it happens).

```py
import numpy as np
```

I looked into this, and yeah, the else block can go. Inspect is still able to get the calling filename even when `__file__` is not found in the frame globals, since it uses a different mechanism (it looks at the importlib loader detail). Using importlib loader to discover the caller is both more direct and more correct, since importlib itself is what sets the `__file__` attribute after importing, so you cut out the middleman this way.

As for why the else block was needed previously, I think it may because of your `ClearNamespaceParser`? The files it failed to inspect the calling filename from were dogfooding examples in .rst which demonstrated the `ClearNamespaceParser` usage. I suppose it kicked `__file__` out of the namespace, which was causing the lookup of `"__file__"` in the frame globals to fail.

I did some checking and found [this](https://github.com/googleapis/python-api-core/blob/c4ff747db17cdeedb0eb2741b308c7b2d2a596fb/google/api_core/page_iterator.py#L210-L249) basically the HTTPIterator has already implemented the logic for paginating over the results and outputting it in one nice iterator
The problem is in this case the parameter max_results is used once as a "page_size" [here](https://github.com/googleapis/python-api-core/blob/c4ff747db17cdeedb0eb2741b308c7b2d2a596fb/google/api_core/page_iterator.py#L402-L426) and once as a "max_results for the iterator" [here](https://github.com/googleapis/python-api-core/blob/c4ff747db17cdeedb0eb2741b308c7b2d2a596fb/google/api_core/page_iterator.py#L387-L389)
I don't know if this behavior is intended or not, but when you pass max_results 2147483647 (max int32) for example, the API doesn't actually reject our API call but just uses the max it allows (although undocumented after further digging found it to be 1000), so maybe we can rename our parameter or change code over at HTTPIterator or just do the pagination ourselves although it seems a bit odd, IDK whats the best practice here, please share some ideas so I'll know what's the best solution here

There are arguments for both capacity and balance.

Pro capacity:
* Most payments succeed, so this is usually the more likely/more up to date state

Pro balance:
* This is the only really confirmed state
* There is no way to influence the fees by starting payments to change the capacity of the mediator. If someone is crazy enough to invest the effort, they could first make a payment to move the mediator's capacity in one direction, then send a second payment to reap the decreased fees. He could make the first payment fail and still keep the reduced fees for the second payment.

I currently favor the balance as the safer bet.

```python
def strip_prefix(string: str, prefix: str) -> str: ...

strip_prefix("hello world", "hello")
strip_prefix("hello world", prefix="hello")
```

Additional behaviors we need to test:

## constructor
- [ ] constructor called with a generator but no children must succeed
- [ ] constructor called with children but no generator must succeed
- [ ] constructor called a generator and no children must throw `TypeError`
- [ ] constructor called with a generator or any children that are not `Keyring`s must throw `TypeError`

## on_encrypt
- [ ] given no generator, `on_encrypt` with no `data_encryption_key` must throw `EncryptKeyError`
- [ ] given a keyring that does nothing (make one of these as a test util) as the generator, `on_encrypt` with no `data_encryption_key` must throw `EncryptKeyError` (or `GenerateKeyError` if you change to that)
- [ ] given a keyring that always adds an EDK (make one of these as a test util) in each of the generator and multiple children, the `on_encrypt` return value must contain `len(children) + (1 if generator else 0)` more encrypted data keys than the input materials (test this for each of: generator but no children, children but no generator, both children and generator)
- [ ] given a keyring that generates but does not add an EDK, and no children, the `on_encrypt` return value must contain exactly the number of EDKs as the input value (test with both no input EDKs and >0)
- [ ] given input materials that already contain a `data_encryption_key`, every keyring must be called (you can use mock keyrings for this to simplify checking)

## on_decrypt
- [ ] given decryption materials that already contain a `data_encryption_key`, we must not call any of the keyrings (you can use mock keyrings for this to simplify checking)
- [ ] given a keyring that never adds an EDK in each of the generator and mutliple children, every keyring must be called (you can use mock keyrings for this to simplify checking)
- [ ] given decryption materials that do *not* already contain a `data_encryption_key`, once a keyring that adds an EDK is encountered, we must not call any further keyrings (you can use mock keyrings for this to simplify checking)

One good starting point when thinking about what to test is to step through your code, and any time you encounter a different behavior, make sure that you are testing that. One metric to look at to start measuring what you have and have not tested is to look at the coverage report and look for lines *and logic branches* that are not covered.

Coverage is not a perfect metric, but it's a good starting point and a reasonably good negative metric: ie, good coverage numbers don't mean that you have complete tests, but bad coverage numbers do mean you don't have complete tests.

> More fundamentally, do we really need to store secretsprovider state in both places?

I believe that we definitively need it in the state file, because StackReference exists and when you take a reference to another stack you need to be able to deserialize the deployment, which requires information about the secrets provider.  It would not be possible to read this information from the `Pulumi.<stack-name>.yaml` file, as we have no idea where it is (in the case of referencing a different project it may not even be present).

It may be possible to remove the configuration from `Pulumi.<stack-name>.yaml` and just say we always have to fetch the deployment in order to understand secrets provider to use, but this would mean we'd have to do the logical equivalent of `pulumi stack export` when running a subset of `pulumi config` commands, and I'm not sure how we want to do that.

> Are you saying that you always shutdown actors manually?

Yes, the current GC mechanism doesn't work for us.

> What's wrong with just keeping the original actor handle in scope for the lifetime of the actor?

For a long-running job, we exit the driver right after it pulls up the actors (otherwise the driver becomes the single point of failure).  Thus, the original handle is garbage collected when the driver exits.

> How would the actor be able to tell when it should call ray.exit_actor?

Users need to manually shutdown an actor when they think the actor isn't needed any more.

> Would ray.exit_actor just call the actor's __ray_terminate__ method?

I'm thinking of the other way around. E.g.

```
def exit_actor():
   global_worker.raylet_client.disconnect()
   sys.exit(0)

def __ray_terminate__(self):
   ray.exit_actor()
```

You mean the three argument `get_sensitivity`? If the spline interpolation is computed in log-space, then the final function is actually `f(x; p) = exp(spline(x; p))`. Thus the derivative of `f` with respect to the parameter `p` is `exp(spline(x; p)) * dspline/dp(x; p)`. If we already have the value of `spline(x; p)` (which we have in the AMICI simulation loop) we can pass it to the 3-argument version so that it does not get recomputed.

It's at runtime, but this is actually translating the other way from Pulumi -> "Python (mapping table)" names, and it's only done for output types.

This is how output types work in this PR:

When unmarshaling the outputs, dictionary keys are translated using the resource's `translate_output_property()` function (as is the case today). If the `dict` is intended to be an output class, we instantiate the class, passing the _translated_ `dict` to the output classes `__init__()`.

The output classes that we generate for our providers inherit from `dict`, so when we pass the _translated_ `dict` to the output class's `__init__()`, it's actually just copying the _translated_ `dict` into itself (if the output class didn't inherit from `dict`, it saves a reference to the _translated_ `dict` as `self._values`). At this point the object has the _translated_ keys, which is what we want to maintain backwards compatibility. Existing programs accessing the values as a `dict` can continue to do so, using the translated key names.

But these objects now have a nicer way of accessing the values, using the new strongly named/typed Python properties that we've added. These properties need to access the values, and since the keys are the translated keys, our provide codegen also generates a `_translate_property()` method on the output class, which does the same things as the resource's `translate_output_property()`: translates Pulumi -> "Python (mapping table)" names. If we see we have a `_translate_property()`, we call it to translate the key to lookup the value.

Thank you @bukepo . This is just the first step towards fixing the retransmission issue with CSL IE.

This works for simulation CI tests as the following steps(after out-of-sync) are sending packet from SSED to parent which will make them resync again.

I'm preparing a follow up fix to address the retransmission issue. The idea is to set the retransmission bit in `TxInfo` in radio if it is a retransmission(which breaks the synchronization). Then Mac layer will check this bit in `TxInfo` and schedule a data poll transmission right after the retransmission to fix the wrong CSL IE in previous retransmission. In this way, the parent will just be temporary out of sync with its SSED.

For the approach you mentioned, I think it can be used to mitigate the issue. If the last packet from SSED to its parent is within a certain threshold, then it does not need to include CSL IE in data frame to avoid retransmission. Comments?

I don't know what the right thing to do here is. This is our first formatter that uses the std-lib AST instead of `typed-ast`, meaning the interp it's run with does matter.

It is not safe to run the formatter in format mode over the same file, which can happen if we partition by interp constraints and there is a Py2 target and Py3 target referring to the same file. While that's valid with a linter, with a formatter, it could produce different results, and then we could have a conflict when trying to merge the digests that will cause Pants to fail. Generally, running a formatter over the same file twice seems undesirable.

Another option is to throw up our hands and always run with a particular interpreter version, based on the subsystem option like this PR does now. That's easiest to implement, but breaks our general support for mixed interpreter constraints: https://blog.pantsbuild.org/python-3-migrations/

It seems like a non-starter to get this formatter to use typed-ast, given that it's trying to emulate Flake8 itself which uses the std lib AST.

A final option is to not land this if we deem it too hard to get working with interpreter constraints and don't want to break that soft guarantee, but that would make me sad and seems undesirable. Two users have reported they would like this feature, one motivated enough to spend his time on the weekend implementing this (thank you Cristian!)

Any other ideas on how to do this, especially that merge conflict risk with using constraints based on the file in question?

--

> Another option is to throw up our hands and always run with a particular interpreter version

I'm leaning towards this, with a warning in the docs and possibly at runtime that this formatter can likely not be used 100% seamlessly with mixed interpreter constraints. Again, I'd be sad if we did not land this due to one workflow not working robustly, as it does work great for most other use cases.

we have with us in `gradient` function, `gx` i.e. `1/x` as well as `x`.
Since, second derivative of log is `-(gx * gx)` = `-1/(x^2)`. We use the pattern.

Considering `log2` (similar case for `log10`)
we have with us, `gx` i.e. `1/(log(2) * x)` as well as `x`.
Since second derivative is `-1/(log(2) * x * x)`
which we get in the code using `negative(gx * reciprocal(x))`, where `gx=1/(log(2) * x`.

I've been thinking about this a lot this week!

So first, an anecdote: I actually ran into a real case where I would have used `run_process` if we had it. It was a very simple process monitor – I just wanted a script that would start up a flask dev server + a worker daemon that it talked to, and tear them both down if either exited. So:

```python
async def main():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        async def run_process_then_quit(cmd):
            await trio.run_process(cmd)
            nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()
        nursery.start_soon(run_process_then_quit, [...])
        nursery.start_soon(run_process_then_quit, [...])

trio.run(main)
```

Earlier you wrote:

> If you're writing a concurrent app, you might want to run multiple processes at once. That's maybe even _more_ likely than that you'd be careful not to run multiple processes at once. If you run multiple processes at once, it's not really safe to not isolate their I/O from each other.

This makes a lot of sense. But... OTOH, in my example, the correct behavior is actually to not capture stdout/stderr :-). Both daemons are dumping debug chatter on their output, and I definitely want to see all of it on my terminal as it happens. So I guess both capturing and non-capturing use cases do happen in real life, even when you're writing concurrent apps!

OTOH, in this case it probably would be better to do `input=b""`. It doesn't actually matter that much in practice, because these processes never read from their input. But if I was wrong about that, and they actually did, then `input=b""` would make sure I noticed and had to figure out some more reasonable solution. So that to me suggests that maybe we shouldn't be using a single flag to control both input and output handling simultaneously.

I've also seen *not* passing `input=b""` cause problems in real life, because of this kind of incorrect assumption. For example, this fun bug, which was caused because it never occurred to the pip developers that someone would write a `setup.py` that prompted the user for input: https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/2732

One more random thought: I figured out why I'm a bit uncomfortable with either capturing both stdout + stderr or neither. It's because the the original motivation for making them different things was exactly to encourage people to capture stdout for programmatic consumption while leaving stderr pointing at a terminal :-). Specifically, to make unix pipelines work: `foo | bar` sends `foo`'s stdout to `bar`, but `foo`'s stderr stays on the terminal for a human to read.

OK. So how do we decide on defaults.

One good heuristic for defaults is: whatever configuration is most common, should be the default. I guess that's implicitly what we're thinking of when we debate whether concurrent programs normally want to capture or not.

There's another heuristic, to consider too, though: if there are two configurations for two sorts of users, then the default should be the one where the users two don't want it will realize immediately that they have the wrong configuration. I.e., any default will be wrong sometimes, so pick the default that when it's wrong, is obviously wrong.

In this case – the two kinds of users are the ones who want to capture output, and the ones who don't. The ones who want to capture output are going to immediately *do* something with that output – parse it or write it to a file or something. As they type `run_process`, they are thinking about how they're going to capture that output. If our default is to *not* capture output, then they will immediately notice and turn on output capturing. OTOH, the users who don't want to capture output may not be thinking about the output at all. They weren't planning to write any code to handle it. Maybe they expect that the program won't print any output at all. If our default *is* to capture output, and they don't stop and think about that, then they'll end up silently discarding the output. That seems like a nastier failure mode. Especially for the stderr case, where there really is no output for the first month your system is in production, and you don't notice that you're discarding the stderr until after things blow up and you have no idea why.

So I am leaning towards: we should follow `subprocess.run`, and *not* capture stdout/stderr by default.

If we go with this, then we'll need some spelling for how to override the default. For this I'm still leaning towards `capture_stdout=`, `capture_stderr=` as separate switches, because of the intuition from `foo | bar`.

Now, what about the input. Here, the argument works a bit differently... `input=b""` actually seems like the safer default. Having multiple processes reading from the same pipe at the same time is a nightmare; ttys have [some protection](http://curiousthing.org/sigttin-sigttou-deep-dive-linux) but it's still a mess. And even if you're only running one process, you can have fun failure modes like the bug above complaining that "pip just freezes on some packages". So I'm leaning towards `input=b""` as the default.

If we go with this, the nI'm not quite sure how to get the ergonomics right for when people want to override it. In particular:

* what do you write when you want the child to inherit stdin? `input=None`? Not super intuitive, though I guess it's straightforward enough to document and it would get the job done.  `stdin=0`? That'd be pretty arcane.

* How do we resolve conflicts between `input` and `stdin`? I think we want to allow `run_process(cmd, stdin=<arcane subprocess stuff>)`. But if `input=b""` is the default, then there's no way to tell for sure that someone hasn't pass both `input=` and `stdin=`, which is awkward. I guess the best option is just to say, if `input == b""`, and `stdin` was given, then we ignore `input`? Not the most elegant thing, but maybe the alternatives are worse.

There are also other options, like making the default signature `input=None` and declaring that that *means* the same thing as `input=b""` (except possibly if `stdin=` is passed). But that seems like even more of a mess than the alternatives.

What do you think?

The `_trainable_func` method belongs to the `FunctionWrapper` class. As I understand it, it is not part of the `Trainable` class API. In both the old and new implementation, this method is used to dynamically create a `Trainable` class which uses the provided function.

This change is only to simplify how functions are wrapped within a Trainable object. I felt it was clunky and unnecessary to have to call this method "twice", e.g., `_trainable_func()(config, reporter)`. Alternatively, if we would want to keep it such that it returns a function, we could consider making it a property (but then again, this feels unnecessary).

Additionally, I am getting ready to propose a generator based function API which would simplify things greatly. With this new way of wrapping the function, backwards compatibility should be clean and straightforward.

This new implementation of ray's FunctionRunner accounts for these change so shouldn't cause any problems. This should not affect any older versions unless someone tries to unpickle incompatible versions or tries to access the "private" method directly. Even then, I would think it unlikely as the FunctionWrapper instances aren't exposed through the public API and users should only be using one version of ray at a time.

That being said, this change is minor and completely optional. We can revert back to the old way but I am not convinced this is necessary. Given the re-write of `FunctionWrapper`, this would be a good time to change it.

Hi @Tea7ime, sorry for my late answer. I played a bit with the `pdist` function.

```python
def corner_peaks_new(image, min_distance=1, threshold_abs=None,
                     threshold_rel=0.1, exclude_border=True, indices=True,
                     num_peaks=np.inf, footprint=None, labels=None):

    peaks = peak_local_max(image, min_distance=min_distance,
                           threshold_abs=threshold_abs,
                           threshold_rel=threshold_rel,
                           exclude_border=exclude_border,
                           indices=False, num_peaks=num_peaks,
                           footprint=footprint, labels=labels)
    if min_distance > 0:
        coords = np.transpose(peaks.nonzero())
        num_peaks = coords.shape[0]
        # Compute the distance between each detected distance
        dist_mat = pdist(coords, 'chebyshev')
        rejected_peaks = set()
        start = end = 0
        step = num_peaks - 1
        for i in range(num_peaks - 2):
            end += step
            if i in rejected_peaks:
                start = end
                step -= 1
                continue
            rejected_peaks.update(i + 1 + np.nonzero(dist_mat[start:end] <
                                                     min_distance)[0])
            start = end
            step -= 1

        # Remove the peaks that are too close to each others
        rejected_peaks = list(rejected_peaks)
        if indices is True:
            return np.delete(coords, rejected_peaks, axis=0)

        peaks[coords[rejected_peaks, :]] = False

    if indices is True:
        return np.transpose(peaks.nonzero())

    return peaks
```

This implementation is slower then the actual one (but still fast), but it allows to use different distances: the actual implementation corresponds to the Chebyshev distance (it considers a square area around the peaks), but `pdist` allows to use the Euclidean distance (a circle area around the peaks).

I did some of my own research while reviewing this, and I found a confusing behavior. It seem defaults for these specs are not being parsed the way I'd expect. For example, `lgb.DaskLGBMClassifier.fit()` takes 4 arguments. Its `.args` from `inspect.getfullargspec()` correctly has 4 items, but its `.defaults` only has 1!

```python
import inspect
import lightgbm as lgb
dask_spec = inspect.getfullargspec(lgb.DaskLGBMClassifier.fit)
sklearn_spec = inspect.getfullargspec(lgb.LGBMClassifier.fit)

print(dask_spec.args)
# ['self', 'X', 'y', 'sample_weight']

print(dask_spec.defaults)
# (None,)

print(sklearn_spec.args)
# ['self', 'X', 'y', 'sample_weight', 'init_score', 'eval_set', 'eval_names', 'eval_sample_weight', 'eval_init_score', 'eval_metric', 'early_stopping_rounds', 'verbose', 'feature_name', 'categorical_feature', 'callbacks', 'init_model']

print(sklearn_spec.defaults)
# (None, None, None, None, None, None, None, None, True, 'auto', 'auto', None, None)
```

After some investigation, I found that the `.defaults` list is an ordered list of defaults **only for the arguments that have defaults**. This makes it really hard to compare two different functions.

For the comparison, could you try looping over the values of `dask_spec.args`, and then checking that the defaults are the same as the equivalent sklearn default by using `inspect.signature()`?

For example:

```python
dask_params = inspect.signature(lgb.DaskLGBMClassifier.fit).parameters
sklearn_params = inspect.signature(lgb.LGBMClassifier.fit).parameters

print(dask_params['X'].default)
print(sklearn_params['X'].default)

dask_params['X'].default == sklearn_params['X'].default
```

The attribute values in HTML tags can be 'quoted' or "double-quoted" or (historically) not quoted at all.

To match all of these quoting styles, you could use a named capture group that matches any of the beginning quote styles `(?P<q1>"|\'|\b)`and then close the match with a back-reference `(?P=q1)`. However, a named capture group will count among the numbered groups considered by `_html_search_regex()`, so you'd also have to use a named capture group for the JSON that you're targeting `(?P<schema_obj>.+?)` instead of just `{.+)` and add `group='schema_obj'` to the method arguments to tell it to use that group. But I admit it's unlikely Snapchat will change its quoting style.
 
>     * Done
> 
>     * I did not implement this as I do not understand it fully

The HTML tag before the target JSON `<script data-react-helmet="true" type="application/ld\+json">` would be equally valid as `<script type="application/ld\+json" data-react-helmet="true">`. However regular expression syntax is not good at expressing this. The Perl-style REs supported in Python have an 'alternative' syntax `(?(A)match-if-A matched|match-if-A-didn't-match)`, where `A` is a capture group name or number. Thus `(?P<A>A-pattern)B-pattern(?(A)|A-pattern)` matches both `A-patternB-pattern` and `B-patternA-pattern`.

So your match (without any of the other suggested changes) could be `r'<script (?P<helmet>data-react-helmet="true" )type="application/ld\+json"(?(helmet)| data-react-helmet="true")>(.+)</script>'`

Introducing a named capture group means tweaking the method call as above, and again it's probably unlikely that Snapchat will change the order of the attributes, unless they upgrade their React toolset to something that accidentally generates a different order.

```suggestion
    def from_pretrained_transformer(
        cls, model_name: str, namespace: str = "tokens", oov_token: Optional[str] = None
    ) -> "Vocabulary":
        """
        Initialize a vocabulary from the vocabulary of a pretrained transformer model.
        If `oov_token` is not given, we will try to infer it from the transformer tokenizer.
        """
        from allennlp.common import cached_transformers

        tokenizer = cached_transformers.get_tokenizer(model_name)
        if oov_token is None:
            if hasattr(tokenizer, "_unk_token"):
                oov_token = tokenizer._unk_token
            elif hasattr(tokenizer, "special_tokens_map"):
                oov_token = tokenizer.special_tokens_map.get("unk_token")
        vocab = cls(non_padded_namespaces=[namespace], oov_token=oov_token)
        vocab.add_transformer_vocab(tokenizer, namespace)
        return vocab
```

This is a bit of a pain - only noticed this as we started to move our own app in to QA with Google Cloud SQL - mysql doesn't support indexes on TextField, it only allows a partial index of the text. Its my fault, I only tested against postgres, sqlite and mariadb in development - mariadb supports indexing `TEXT` fields by hashing the text and indexing that instead.

Even if we decided to add a partial index for mysql, its a real pain to do so with django - you have to add it as a RunSQL migration and tell it that its a partial index. I'm investigating to see whether there is a way to do this operation just on mysql DBs. It would also mean that there is a possibility that the uniqueness constraint would be violated if just the first 255 characters are the same (the limit on a UTF-8 string index in mysql 5.7/5.8). Probably would have to be a non-unique index in that case.

If we can't, we'd have to ship instructions on how to swap the model so that mysql users can create a migration themselves that will work and give instructions for that.

A final, more drastic action would be to not store the ID tokens at all. Currently, we only retrieve ID tokens to validate that an ID token that we've received is valid. It is possible instead to validate them by verifying the JWT token signature, and checking `iss`, `nbf`, and `exp`. Of course, this makes it impossible to revoke an ID token when revoking an access token.

It's not going to be `(800, 1333)`. The `GeneralizedRCNNTransform` maintains the ratio of the image by setting the minimum edge to 800. Then if the maximum edge exceeds 1333 it resizes the image further. So the input size in the network is variable.

If we put `(1, 1)` as the `min_size`, that's technically accurate but it doesn't have the same interpretation as for the classification case. In classification this size depicts the actual size of the smallest image that can go through the network. Here the value `(1, 1)` doesn't tell us anything about the network; it just tells us that we can throw any size in because we will resize it eventually to at least 800.

I'm going to suggest a rewrite which is optional, but which I think makes this test easier to read.

`parametrize` can handle tuple unpacking into named arguments, and if we use `run_line(<list>)`, then we can take the options as an array of args instead of doing the `if arg` "slamming" to assemble a string.

```suggestion
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "add_args, field_name, expected_value",
    [
        (["--high-assurance"], "is_high_assurance", True),
        (["--no-high-assurance"], "is_high_assurance", False),
        (["--authentication-timeout", "100"], "authentication_assurance_timeout", 100),
        (["--visibility", "authenticated"], "group_visibility", "authenticated"),
        (["--members-visibility", "members"], "group_members_visibility", "members"),
        (["--join-requests"], "join_requests", True),
        (["--no-join-requests"], "join_requests", False),
        (["--signup-fields", "address"], "signup_fields", ["address"]),
        (
            ["--signup-fields", "address1,address2"],
            "signup_fields",
            ["address1", "address2"],
        ),
    ],
)
def test_group_set_policies(run_line, add_args, field_name, expected_value):
    fixtures = load_response_set("get_and_set_group_policies")
    group_id = fixtures.metadata["group_id"]

    result = run_line(["globus", "group", "set-policies", group_id] + add_args)
```
